clean_story:
In-memory data. Microsecond speed.

Redis (Remote Dictionary Server) is named for its job: a dictionary that holds keys and values, but in RAM—not on slow disks.

Most databases fetch data like a librarian climbing shelves. Redis grabs it like a waiter’s notepad—already in hand. It stores everything in memory, cutting read/write times from milliseconds to microseconds. Need a leaderboard? Redis sorts scores instantly. Tracking live chat messages? It streams them without lag.

Its secret: five core data structures (strings, lists, sets, hashes, sorted sets), each optimized for a task. A "sorted set" can rank 10 million users by score in 20ms. Companies like Twitter use it to count likes in real time; game servers track player stats without stuttering.

No magic—just precision. Data expires automatically (like a self-cleaning whiteboard). Replication copies writes to backup servers in under a second. Failover switches masters faster than a heartbeat.

Three related subjects are cache stampedes, eventual consistency models, and how RAM shapes software speed.
